title: Structural Equivalence of Bayesian Meta-Analysis and Bayesian Survey Inference
text:
Preface
Imagine you are a meta-analyst. You have collected 117 studies on brief alcohol interventions for adolescents, and many of these studies report multiple effect sizes — different outcome measures, different follow-up times, different subgroups. You fit a Bayesian random-effects model in Stan, specifying a compound-symmetric working covariance with a working correlation of \(\rho = 0.8\) to account for the dependence among effect sizes within studies. The sampler converges, the posterior summaries look reasonable, and you report credible intervals for the overall effect and for each study-specific effect.
But then someone asks: which of those intervals can you trust?
You chose \(\rho = 0.8\) as a sensitivity parameter. The true within-study correlation is unknown. The compound-symmetric structure is a convenient working assumption, not a verified fact. If the working covariance is misspecified, some of your credible intervals may be too narrow and others too wide. The question is not whether misspecification matters — the sandwich variance estimator, long established in the frequentist robust variance estimation (RVE) literature (Hedges, Tipton, and Johnson 2010; Tipton 2015; Pustejovsky and Tipton 2022), was designed precisely for this situation — but rather which parameters are affected, by how much, and what to do about it. For Bayesian meta-analysts, there has been no systematic answer.
This book provides one.

title: Structural Equivalence of Bayesian Meta-Analysis and Bayesian Survey Inference
text:
The parallel universe
The problem I just described — Bayesian inference under a misspecified working model for clustered data — is not new. It has been studied intensively in another branch of statistics: survey inference.
When a complex survey samples individuals within primary sampling units (PSUs), the within-cluster dependence inflates the variance of population-level estimates relative to what a naive model would predict. The classical design effect (Kish 1965) quantifies this inflation. In the frequentist tradition, the sandwich variance estimator (Binder 1983; Liang and Zeger 1986) — or its survey-specific incarnation, the linearization estimator — corrects for the misspecification. But what about the Bayesian analyst who fits a hierarchical model to survey data and reports posterior credible intervals? Those intervals are computed from the working-model posterior, which may not account for the complex sampling design.
Williams and Savitsky (2021) solved this problem by developing the Cholesky Calibration of Credible intervals (CCC), a post-processing method that recalibrates MCMC draws so that the resulting credible intervals achieve frequentist coverage consistent with the sandwich variance. Savitsky and Toth (2016) and Williams and Savitsky (2020) laid the groundwork by establishing theoretical properties of the pseudo-posterior under informative sampling. And in my own work (Lee 2026b), I developed Design Effect Ratios (DERs) — per-parameter diagnostics that tell the analyst exactly which parameters are sensitive to the survey design and which are already calibrated.
These tools — the CCC and the DER framework — are precisely what the meta-analyst needs. But meta-analysts do not read the survey literature, and survey statisticians do not read the meta-analysis literature. The two communities — one descending from Hedges and Olkin (1985), the other from Kish (1965) — have been solving the same problem independently, using different terminology for the same mathematical objects: “studies” and “PSUs” for the same clustering structure, “effect sizes” and “observations” for the same level-1 units, “heterogeneity variance” and “between-cluster variance” for the same parameter \(\sigma^2_\theta\).

title: Structural Equivalence of Bayesian Meta-Analysis and Bayesian Survey Inference
text:
The bridge insight
The univariate connection between these two worlds has been understood for decades. Efron and Morris (1975) demonstrated that the James–Stein shrinkage estimator — a cornerstone of empirical Bayes methodology — is the same mathematical object whether one arrives at it from a random-effects meta-analytic perspective or from a small-area estimation perspective. The Fay–Herriot model (Fay and Herriot 1979), the workhorse of small-area estimation in survey statistics, is structurally identical to the univariate random-effects meta-analytic model. When every study contributes exactly one effect size (\(n_j = 1\) for all \(j\)), the compound-symmetric working covariance is vacuous, the design effect equals one, and there is nothing left to prove.
But modern meta-analysis rarely lives in the univariate world. A typical research synthesis contains studies that report multiple outcomes, multiple treatment contrasts, or multiple subgroup estimates. The dataset I mentioned earlier — Tanner-Smith and Lipsey (2015) — has \(J = 117\) studies contributing \(N = 1{,}198\) effect sizes, with an average of about 10 per study. In this multivariate setting, the within-study dependence is nontrivial, the design effect is no longer one, and the question of which credible intervals to trust becomes urgent.
Extending the univariate bridge to this multivariate case — proving that the structural equivalence persists when studies contribute dependent observations, and that the survey tools for diagnosing and correcting working-model misspecification transfer exactly to meta-analysis — is the contribution of this book. The extension is not obvious. It requires identifying the precise conditions under which the two likelihoods coincide, tracking the consequences through the sandwich variance estimator and the posterior distribution, and deriving closed-form expressions for the Design Effect Ratio in terms of meta-analytic quantities. It requires, in short, building a complete mathematical theory.
What emerges from that theory is stronger than an analogy. The mapping preserves not only the likelihood but the posterior, the sandwich variance, and the design-effect diagnostic — each object in one framework has an exact counterpart in the other. These are exact results: under the five conditions, the two models are a single mathematical object expressed in two notational systems.

title: Structural Equivalence of Bayesian Meta-Analysis and Bayesian Survey Inference
text:
What this book proves
The theory rests on five exactness conditions — compound symmetry (E1), cluster-specific variance identification (E2), unit weights (E3), a DER simplification condition (E4), and normal level-1 errors (E5) — that specify when the structural equivalence is exact. Four main results follow.
Theorem 1 (Likelihood Isomorphism). Under conditions (E1)–(E3) and (E5), the meta-analytic log-likelihood and the survey pseudo-log-likelihood differ by an additive constant that does not depend on the parameter vector \(\boldsymbol{\phi} = (\boldsymbol{\beta}^\intercal, \boldsymbol{\theta}^\intercal)^\intercal\). The two fields have been optimizing the same objective function. This isomorphism is realized by a constructive map \(\mathcal{T}\) that sends the survey error variance to the meta-analytic sampling variance (\(\sigma^2_{e,j} \mapsto \bar{s}_j^2\)) and the survey weights to unity (\(\tilde{w}_{ij} \mapsto 1\)).
Theorem 2 (Sandwich Equivalence). The sandwich variance estimators are numerically identical: \(\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{sand}}^{\mathrm{surv}} = \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{sand}}^{\mathrm{meta}}\). This follows directly from the likelihood isomorphism, because the scores, Hessians, and meat matrices all coincide when the likelihoods differ by a constant. In particular, the survey HC sandwich and the meta-analytic CR sandwich are the same mathematical object.
Corollary 1 (Posterior Equivalence). The survey pseudo-posterior and the meta-analytic working-model posterior are identical as probability measures. The constant \(e^c\) cancels in Bayes’ formula. Any MCMC algorithm applied to one framework generates draws from the other.
Corollary 2 (CCC Equivalence). The Cholesky-corrected posterior draws are identical in distribution across the two frameworks. The CCC, developed in the survey context by Williams and Savitsky (2021), transfers directly to meta-analysis.
Beyond these four equivalence results, the book develops the DER Transfer Theorem (Theorem 3), which provides closed-form decompositions of the Design Effect Ratio for both the overall effect (\(\mu\)) and the study-specific effects (\(\theta_j\)). For fixed-effect parameters identified by between-study variation, the DER decomposes as \(\mathrm{DER}_{\beta_k} = \mathrm{DEFF} \cdot (1 - R_k)\), where \(\mathrm{DEFF}\) is the classical design effect and \(R_k\) is an information fraction derived from the Schur complement of the posterior Hessian. For the study-specific effects, \(\mathrm{DER}_{\theta_j}^{\mathrm{cond}} = B_j \cdot \mathrm{DEFF}_j\), where \(B_j\) is the posterior retention weight — the complement of the shrinkage factor.

title: Structural Equivalence of Bayesian Meta-Analysis and Bayesian Survey Inference
text:
The conservation law
The result I find most striking is the conservation law (Theorem 3(c)). In the balanced intercept-only model:
\[
\mathrm{DER}_\mu + \mathrm{DER}_\theta^{\mathrm{cond}} = \mathrm{DEFF}.
\]
The total design-effect budget is conserved. Within-study dependence creates a fixed amount of design sensitivity, equal to \(\mathrm{DEFF}\), that is redistributed between the overall effect and the study-specific effects by hierarchical pooling but is never created or destroyed. When pooling is strong (\(B\) small), most of the budget migrates to \(\mu\): the overall effect’s credible interval is severely miscalibrated, but the study-level posteriors are approximately correct. When pooling is weak (\(B\) large, as is typical in meta-analysis), the budget migrates to \(\theta_j\): the study-level credible intervals — the ones that appear in the forest plot — carry the burden of the design effect, while the overall effect remains approximately calibrated.
This is not merely an algebraic curiosity. It has direct implications for practice. In the Tanner-Smith and Lipsey (2015) data, the empirical DER for \(\mu\) is approximately 1.01 (Tier T1: calibrated), while the mean DER for the study-specific effects is approximately 5.09 (Tier T4: severe under-dispersion). A meta-analyst who reports only the overall effect may conclude that the working correlation does not matter. A meta-analyst who reports study-level predictions — as is increasingly common — faces a serious calibration problem. The conservation law explains why: the design-effect budget has been redistributed, not eliminated.

title: Structural Equivalence of Bayesian Meta-Analysis and Bayesian Survey Inference
text:
Calibrated Bayes
The intellectual framework that unifies these results is what Little (2004) and Little (2012) called “Calibrated Bayes” — the idea that Bayesian models should be evaluated by whether their posterior credible intervals achieve adequate frequentist coverage. Rubin (1984) articulated the philosophical foundation: Bayesian computation is a powerful engine for inference, but its outputs should be “calibrated” against repeated-sampling properties when those properties are relevant to the scientific question.
The DER makes Calibrated Bayes operational at the individual-parameter level for meta-analysis. Rather than asking “is this model well calibrated?” in the aggregate, the analyst can ask “is this specific parameter well calibrated?” and receive a quantitative answer. The five-tier classification system developed in Lee (2026b) — ranging from T0 (over-dispersed) through T1 (calibrated) to T4 (severe under-dispersion) — provides actionable guidance: parameters in T1 need no correction; parameters in T2 should be reported with their DER values; parameters in T3–T4 should be corrected via the CCC.

title: Structural Equivalence of Bayesian Meta-Analysis and Bayesian Survey Inference
text:
The practical workflow
One of the most important implications of the structural equivalence is that the tools developed in the survey literature can be applied to existing meta-analytic models without refitting. Any Bayesian meta-analysis conducted in Stan — whether using the CE or CHE working model — already computes the survey pseudo-posterior. The DER can be computed from the MCMC output by extracting scores and Hessians from Stan’s generated quantities block and constructing the sandwich variance. The CCC is a pure post-processing step: it transforms the existing MCMC draws by an affine map, requiring only the Cholesky factors of the posterior covariance and the sandwich variance.
The practical workflow is three steps: compute the DER for each parameter; classify the result using the five-tier system; correct those parameters that fall in Tiers T3–T4 using the CCC. No new sampler, no new prior, no refitting. This “compute–classify–correct” pipeline is the applied payoff of the structural equivalence.

title: Structural Equivalence of Bayesian Meta-Analysis and Bayesian Survey Inference
text:
Bidirectional transfer
The structural equivalence is not a one-way import. From survey inference to meta-analysis, three tools cross the bridge: the Design Effect Ratio as a per-parameter diagnostic, the Cholesky Calibration Correction as a post-MCMC recalibration, and more broadly, the design-effect way of thinking about working-model misspecification — viewing variance distortion as a structured, interpretable quantity rather than a nuisance.
From meta-analysis back to survey inference, the conservation law is genuinely new. The decomposition \(\mathrm{DER}_\mu + \mathrm{DER}_\theta^{\mathrm{cond}} = \mathrm{DEFF}\) was not known in the survey literature; it reveals that the total design effect is a fixed budget redistributed by hierarchical pooling. The conditional–marginal sandwich distinction that emerges from the DER decomposition clarifies a subtlety that had not been explicitly articulated in survey design-effect research.
The empirical illustration sharpens the point. In the Tanner-Smith and Lipsey (2015) data, the DER follows a three-step gradient across the parameter hierarchy: within-study moderators are essentially calibrated (mean DER \(\approx 0.97\)), between-study moderators show mild sensitivity (mean DER \(\approx 1.11\)), and control covariates exhibit substantial under-dispersion (mean DER \(\approx 2.8\)–\(3.0\)). This gradient, discovered in a meta-analytic application, has direct implications for survey practitioners who face analogous parameter hierarchies — and the equivalence framework makes the connection precise enough to investigate.

title: Structural Equivalence of Bayesian Meta-Analysis and Bayesian Survey Inference
text:
How to read this book
The book is organized in five parts.
Part I (Chapters 1–3) establishes the foundations: notation and conventions that unify the terminology of the two fields; complete model definitions for the CE, HE, and CHE models; and the five exactness conditions that govern the structural equivalence, including a detailed analysis of what happens when each condition is violated.
Part II (Chapters 4–6) contains the core equivalence results. Chapter 4 proves the likelihood isomorphism (Theorem 1) in two cases — the independence working model (\(\rho = 0\)) and the compound-symmetric working model (\(\rho > 0\)). Chapter 5 proves the sandwich equivalence (Theorem 2) and establishes that the survey HC sandwich and the meta-analytic CR sandwich are the same object. Chapter 6 derives the posterior equivalence (Corollary 1) and the CCC equivalence (Corollary 2).
Part III (Chapters 7–9) develops the DER framework. Chapter 7 defines the Design Effect Ratio, proves the DER transfer theorem (Theorem 3), and introduces the five-tier classification system. Chapter 8 presents the conservation law and its economic interpretation. Chapter 9 analyzes boundary behavior — what happens as \(\sigma^2_\theta \to 0\), \(\sigma^2_\theta \to \infty\), or \(\rho_{\mathrm{true}} \to 0\).
Part IV (Chapters 10–12) addresses extensions and subtleties. Chapter 10 extends the results to observation-level covariates (E4-relaxed CE) and to the CHE model. Chapter 11 catalogs the regularity conditions required for the sandwich estimator to be consistent. Chapter 12 treats interpretive issues, including the critical distinction between conditional and marginal DER.
Part V (Chapter 13) discusses the broader implications of the structural equivalence for meta-analytic practice, identifies limitations of the current framework, and outlines directions for future research including simulation studies, non-Gaussian extensions, and software implementation.
Different readers may want different paths through the material. A theorist interested in the equivalence proof can read Chapters 1, 3, and 4–6 and stop. An applied meta-analyst who wants to use the DER should read Chapters 1, 7, 8, and 12, with the proofs as optional background. A survey statistician curious about the connection to meta-analysis will find Chapters 2–3 the most illuminating, as they map meta-analytic concepts to survey concepts and vice versa.

title: Structural Equivalence of Bayesian Meta-Analysis and Bayesian Survey Inference
text:
Companion papers
This book is the mathematical backbone of a research program with two companion papers. Lee (2026a) develops a Bayesian hierarchical hurdle beta-binomial model for survey-weighted bounded counts, applying the CCC and DER framework to a substantive analysis of childcare enrollment in the National Household Education Survey. Lee (2026b) introduces the DER framework itself, proves its theoretical properties in the general Bayesian survey setting, and provides empirical illustrations with complex survey data. The present book extends the DER framework from survey inference to meta-analysis via the structural equivalence.
The relationship among the three works is as follows. Lee (2026b) provides the general theory of the DER, with conditions and proofs tailored to the survey context. This book proves that the survey conditions map onto the meta-analytic setting under the five exactness conditions, so that every DER result transfers. Lee (2026a) demonstrates the practical implementation in a full applied analysis, providing the computational patterns — Stan generated quantities for scores, R functions for sandwich computation, the CCC post-processing step — that meta-analysts can adapt for their own work.
JoonHo Lee
March 2026

title: 1  Notation and Conventions
text:
1.1 Index System
This chapter provides a comprehensive reference for every symbol, index, parameter, matrix, and derived quantity used in the theory of structural equivalence between Bayesian meta-analysis and Bayesian survey inference. All notation is defined once here and used consistently throughout subsequent chapters. Where the meta-analytic and survey literatures use different names for the same mathematical object, the unified symbol is given alongside both field-specific terms.
The theory operates over a two-level clustered data structure. Four index variables appear throughout.

title: 1  Notation and Conventions
text:
NoteTerminological Convention



Throughout this book, the terms “study” (meta-analysis), “PSU” or “cluster” (survey), and “group” (general) are used interchangeably to denote the level-2 unit indexed by \(j\). Similarly, “effect size” (meta-analysis) and “observation” (survey) both refer to the level-1 unit indexed by \(i\) within \(j\).





Table 1.1: Index system.












Symbol
Range
Meta-Analytic Meaning
Survey Meaning
Notes




\(j\)
\(1, \ldots, J\)
Studies
Primary sampling units (PSUs) / clusters
Top-level independent units


\(i\)
\(1, \ldots, n_j\)
Effect sizes within study \(j\)
Observations within cluster \(j\)
Cluster size \(n_j\) may vary across \(j\)


\(k\)
\(1, \ldots, p\)
Meta-regression coefficient index
Survey regression coefficient index
Indexes the fixed-effect parameters \(\beta_k\)


\(s\)
\(1, \ldots, S\)
MCMC draw index
MCMC draw index
Post-estimation; \(S\) is the total number of posterior draws







1.1.1 Derived Index Quantities
The following aggregate quantities are derived from the index system:



Table 1.2: Derived index quantities.










Symbol
Definition
Meaning




\(N\)
\(\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{J} n_j\)
Total number of observations across all clusters


\(\bar{n}\)
\(N / J\)
Average cluster size


\(d\)
\(p + J\)
Total dimension of the combined parameter vector \(\boldsymbol{\phi}\)












NoteConvention on Cluster Size



Throughout the theory, we distinguish three regimes:

Balanced designs: \(n_j = n\) for all \(j\), so \(N = nJ\) and \(\bar{n} = n\). Closed-form results (e.g., the conservation law) are stated in this regime.
Unbalanced designs: \(n_j\) varies across \(j\). Formulas involving averages (e.g., \(\overline{\mathrm{DER}_\theta}\)) require careful treatment due to Jensen’s inequality effects.
Singleton clusters: \(n_j = 1\) for all \(j\). This reduces to the univariate random-effects meta-analytic model (Fay–Herriot model in the survey context). Compound symmetry becomes vacuous, and \(\mathrm{DEFF}_j = 1\).

Table 1.4: Variance components.

\(\sigma^2_\theta\)
\(> 0\)
Between-study heterogeneity variance (often written \(\tau^2\) in meta-analysis)

Within-cluster heterogeneity (CHE extension)












ImportantNotation Convention: \(\sigma^2_\theta\) vs. \(\tau^2\)



The meta-analytic literature almost universally uses \(\tau^2\) for between-study heterogeneity. We use \(\sigma^2_\theta\) throughout to (i) maintain notational consistency with the survey literature, (ii) avoid collision with the DER classification threshold notation, and (iii) make the parametric role transparent: \(\sigma^2_\theta\) is the variance of the random effects \(\theta_j\). The correspondence is \(\sigma^2_\theta \equiv \tau^2\).

Table 1.6: Sampling and error variance notation.

Sampling variance for effect size \((i,j)\)

—

Known (conditioned upon)

\(= \bar{s}_j^2\) under (E2)

Cluster-specific within-cluster error variance












ImportantKey Correction: Cluster-Specific Variance Identification



The exactness condition (E2) requires cluster-specific identification \(\sigma^2_{e,j} = \bar{s}_j^2\), not a single global \(\sigma^2_e\) matched to all studies simultaneously. When the within-study average sampling variances \(\bar{s}_j^2\) vary across studies (which is the typical case in meta-analysis), exact isomorphism demands that the survey error variance be matched study-by-study. If a common \(\sigma^2_e\) is used on the survey side while \(\bar{s}_j^2\) varies across \(j\) on the meta-analytic side, the quadratic forms in the two likelihoods differ by a \(\boldsymbol{\phi}\)-dependent term, breaking exact isomorphism. See Chapter 3 for full details.

—
Inclusion probability for observation \((i,j)\)












NoteConvention: Weights in Meta-Analysis



In standard meta-analysis, no design weights appear: each effect size enters the likelihood with implicit unit weight. Under exactness condition (E3), \(\tilde{w}_{ij} = 1\) for all \((i,j)\), aligning the survey pseudo-likelihood with the standard meta-analytic likelihood. When (E3) is violated, the two frameworks target different estimands.




1.3.5 Correlation Parameters



Table 1.9: Correlation parameters.











Symbol
Range
Meaning
Role




\(\rho\)
\([0, 1)\)
Analyst-chosen working within-study/within-cluster correlation
Sensitivity parameter in the CE working model; working ICC in the survey formulation


\(\rho_{\mathrm{true}}\)
\([0, 1)\)
True within-study correlation
Enters the DEFF formula; generally unknown


\(\rho_j^{\mathrm{CE}}\)
\([0, 1)\)
CE-implied marginal ICC: \(\sigma^2_\theta / (\bar{s}_j^2 + \sigma^2_\theta)\)
Derived from the hierarchical CE model after integrating out \(\theta_j\); not the same as working \(\rho\)


\(\rho_j^{\mathrm{CHE}}\)
\([0, 1)\)
CHE-implied marginal ICC: \(\sigma^2_\theta / (\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2 + \sigma^2_\theta)\)
Strictly less than \(\rho_j^{\mathrm{CE}}\) when \(\omega^2 > 0\)












NoteConvention: Study-Specific Correlation and DEFF



These quantities should not be conflated: \(\rho\) is the analyst-chosen working correlation inside \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(\rho)\), whereas \(\rho_j^{\mathrm{CE}}\) and \(\rho_j^{\mathrm{CHE}}\) are model-implied marginal ICCs obtained after integrating over the random effects.
The quantities \(\rho_j^{\mathrm{CE}} = \sigma^2_\theta / (\bar{s}_j^2 + \sigma^2_\theta)\) and \(\rho_j^{\mathrm{CHE}} = \sigma^2_\theta / (\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2 + \sigma^2_\theta)\) are study-specific because \(\bar{s}_j^2\) varies across studies. Similarly, the study-specific DEFF is \(\mathrm{DEFF}_j = 1 + (n_j - 1)\rho_j\). When the context is clear or when discussing average/typical values, we sometimes drop the \(j\) subscript and write \(\rho^{\mathrm{CE}}\), \(\rho^{\mathrm{CHE}}\), and \(\mathrm{DEFF}\) without ambiguity.

title: 1  Notation and Conventions
text:
1.4 Matrices and Operators

1.4.1 Structural Matrices



Table 1.10: Structural matrices.










Symbol
Dimension
Definition




\(\mathbf{I}_{n_j}\)
\(n_j \times n_j\)
Identity matrix


\(\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\)
\(n_j \times 1\)
Vector of ones: \((1, \ldots, 1)^{\intercal}\)


\(\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}\)
\(n_j \times n_j\)
Matrix of ones (all entries equal to 1)








1.4.2 Covariance Matrices



Table 1.11: Covariance matrices.











Symbol
Dimension
Definition
Context




\(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(\rho)\)
\(n_j \times n_j\)
\(\bar{s}_j^2\left[(1-\rho)\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \rho\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}\right]\)
CE working covariance (compound-symmetric)


\(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{CHE}}\)
\(n_j \times n_j\)
\((\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2)\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \sigma^2_\theta\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}\)
CHE marginal covariance (after integrating out \(\theta_j\))


\(\mathrm{Cov}(\mathbf{y}_j)\)
\(n_j \times n_j\)
\(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(\rho) + \sigma^2_\theta\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}\)
CE marginal covariance (after integrating out \(\theta_j\))


\(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\)
\(d \times d\)
\(\mathrm{Cov}_\pi(\boldsymbol{\phi})\); posterior covariance of MCMC draws
Empirical covariance from \(S\) posterior draws








1.4.3 Sandwich Components



Table 1.12: Sandwich variance components.











Symbol
Dimension
Definition
Name




\(\mathbf{s}_j(\boldsymbol{\phi})\)
\(d \times 1\)
\(\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\phi}} \ell_j(\boldsymbol{\phi})\)
Cluster-level score vector


\(\mathbf{H}\)
\(d \times d\)
\(-\nabla^2_{\boldsymbol{\phi}} \ell(\boldsymbol{\phi})\big|_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}}}\)
Negative Hessian (observed information; “bread”)


\(\mathbf{H}^{\mathrm{post}}\)
\(d \times d\)
\(\mathbf{H} + \mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{prior}}\)
Posterior Hessian (includes prior curvature)


\(\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{prior}}\)
\(d \times d\)
\(\mathrm{diag}(\mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{prior}}^{\boldsymbol{\beta}},\, \sigma_\theta^{-2}\mathbf{I}_J)\)
Prior contribution to the Hessian


\(\mathbf{M}\)
\(d \times d\)
\(\sum_{j=1}^{J} \mathbf{s}_j(\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}})\,\mathbf{s}_j(\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}})^{\intercal}\)
Cluster-level score outer-product sum (“meat”)


\(\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{sand}}\)
\(d \times d\)
\(\mathbf{H}^{-1}\,\mathbf{M}\,\mathbf{H}^{-1}\)
Sandwich variance estimator


\(\mathbf{r}_j\)
\(n_j \times 1\)
\(\mathbf{y}_j - \mathbf{X}_j\boldsymbol{\beta} - \theta_j\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\)
Cluster-level residual vector












ImportantConvention: Likelihood-Only Scores in the Meat



The meat matrix \(\mathbf{M}\) always uses likelihood-only cluster scores \(\mathbf{s}_j(\boldsymbol{\phi}) = \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\phi}} \ell_j(\boldsymbol{\phi})\), not posterior scores that include prior contributions. The bread may optionally use the posterior Hessian \(\mathbf{H}^{\mathrm{post}} = \mathbf{H} + \mathbf{H}_{\mathrm{prior}}\), following Williams and Savitsky (2021). This distinction is critical for correct implementation of both the sandwich variance and the Cholesky correction.




1.4.4 Cholesky Factors



Table 1.13: Cholesky factors for the CCC transformation.










Symbol
Definition
Role




\(\mathbf{L}_1\)
\(\mathrm{chol}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathrm{MCMC}})\)
Lower Cholesky factor of the posterior covariance


\(\mathbf{L}_2\)
\(\mathrm{chol}(\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{sand}})\)
Lower Cholesky factor of the sandwich variance








1.4.5 Hessian Block Structure
For the combined parameter \(\boldsymbol{\phi} = (\boldsymbol{\beta}^{\intercal}, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\intercal})^{\intercal}\), the Hessian has a \(2 \times 2\) block partition:
\[
\mathbf{H} = \begin{pmatrix}
\mathbf{H}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}\boldsymbol{\beta}} & \mathbf{H}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}\boldsymbol{\theta}} \\
\mathbf{H}_{\boldsymbol{\theta}\boldsymbol{\beta}} & \mathbf{H}_{\boldsymbol{\theta}\boldsymbol{\theta}}
\end{pmatrix},
\]
where \(\mathbf{H}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) is \(p \times p\), \(\mathbf{H}_{\boldsymbol{\theta}\boldsymbol{\theta}}\) is \(J \times J\), and \(\mathbf{H}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}\boldsymbol{\theta}}\) is \(p \times J\). The Schur complement with respect to the random effects is:
\[
\mathbf{S}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{H}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \mathbf{H}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}\boldsymbol{\theta}}\,\mathbf{H}_{\boldsymbol{\theta}\boldsymbol{\theta}}^{-1}\,\mathbf{H}_{\boldsymbol{\theta}\boldsymbol{\beta}}.
\]
This Schur complement governs identifiability (regularity condition C7) and the fixed-effect DER decomposition.

title: 1  Notation and Conventions
text:
1.5 Derived Quantities: DER Framework

1.5.1 Design Effect and Design Effect Ratio



Table 1.14: Design effect quantities.










Symbol
Definition
Interpretation




\(\mathrm{DEFF}\)
\(1 + (\bar{n} - 1)\rho_{\mathrm{true}}\)
Classical design effect (average across clusters)


\(\mathrm{DEFF}_j\)
\(1 + (n_j - 1)\rho_{\mathrm{true}}\)
Study-specific design effect for cluster \(j\)


\(\mathrm{DER}_k\)
\([\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{sand}}]_{kk} \big/ [\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathrm{MCMC}}]_{kk}\)
Design Effect Ratio for parameter \(\phi_k\)












NoteThree-Regime Interpretation of DER




\(\mathrm{DER}_k = 1\): The working-model posterior is calibrated; no correction needed.
\(\mathrm{DER}_k > 1\): The posterior is under-dispersed (credible intervals too narrow); the working model understates uncertainty.
\(\mathrm{DER}_k < 1\): The posterior is over-dispersed (credible intervals too wide); the working model overstates uncertainty.





1.5.2 Shrinkage and Information Quantities



Table 1.15: Shrinkage and information quantities.











Symbol
Definition
Range
Meaning




\(B_j\)
\(\displaystyle\frac{\sigma^2_\theta}{\sigma^2_\theta + \bar{s}_j^2 / n_j}\)
\((0, 1)\)
Posterior retention weight on the study-specific estimate for study \(j\)


\(B\)
\(\displaystyle\frac{\sigma^2_\theta}{\sigma^2_\theta + \bar{s}^2 / n}\)
\((0, 1)\)
Common retention weight in the balanced case


\(\kappa_j(J)\)
\(\displaystyle 1 - \frac{1}{J(1-B_j) + B_j}\)
\((0, 1)\)
Finite-cluster coupling factor for study \(j\)


\(R_k\)
\(1 - [ (\mathbf{H}^{\mathrm{post}}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}\boldsymbol{\beta}})^{-1}]_{kk} / [ (\mathbf{S}^{\mathrm{post}}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}})^{-1}]_{kk}\)
\([0, 1]\)
Canonical Schur-complement identifying-variation fraction for coefficient \(\beta_k\). Under (E4) and balanced designs, \(R_k = B\); in general unbalanced designs, \(R_k\) may differ from \(B\) (see Definition 7)


\(\gamma_k\)
\(\displaystyle\frac{\mathrm{Var}_{\mathrm{between}}(\mathbf{x}_{jk})}{\mathrm{Var}_{\mathrm{total}}(\mathbf{x}_{ijk})}\)
\([0, 1]\)
Heuristic between-study fraction of covariate variation (E4-relaxed CE summary)


\(\alpha\)
\(n / \bar{s}^2\)
\((0, \infty)\)
Within-study precision (balanced case); used in Schur complement calculations












NoteConvention on \(R_k\) and \(\gamma_k\)



For theorem-level statements, \(R_k\) and \(\gamma_k\) should be understood as information-fraction quantities derived from the Schur complement of the posterior Hessian, not merely raw variance ratios. The variance-ratio formulas given above are exact under balanced designs with orthogonal covariates. In general unbalanced meta-regression, the information-based definition is:
\[
R_k = \frac{[\mathbf{S}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{\mathrm{post}\,-1}]_{kk} - [\mathbf{H}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{\mathrm{post}\,-1}]_{kk}}{[\mathbf{S}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{\mathrm{post}\,-1}]_{kk}},
\]
where \(\mathbf{S}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{\mathrm{post}}\) is the Schur complement of the posterior Hessian \(\mathbf{H}^{\mathrm{post}}\) with respect to the random effects. Under a flat \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) prior (\(h_\mu = 0\)), \(R_\mu = B\) exactly for the intercept in balanced designs. Under a proper \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) prior (\(h_\mu > 0\)), \(R_\mu = J\alpha B / (J\alpha + h_\mu)\), which is asymptotically exact (\(R_\mu \to B\)) under condition (C6) (\(J\alpha \gg h_\mu\)). The relationship \(R_k \approx \gamma_k B\) is a balanced-design heuristic; the Schur-complement definition above is the canonical one in general.
This covariance-based definition is the canonical definition used throughout the theory. It is the only version that is sign-correct (yielding \(R_k \in [0, B]\)) and generalizes properly to unbalanced multivariate settings. Other formulations (e.g., using raw information blocks \([\mathbf{S}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{\mathrm{post}}]_{kk}/[\mathbf{H}_{\boldsymbol{\beta}\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{\mathrm{post}}]_{kk}\), or products of inverse and non-inverse blocks) may fail to preserve the correct sign or reduce to \(B\) in the balanced scalar case. See Definition 7 in Chapter 7 for the formal definition.

title: 1  Notation and Conventions
text:
1.6 CHE-Specific Derived Quantities
All CE quantities have CHE counterparts obtained via the substitution \(\bar{s}_j^2 \mapsto \bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2\):



Table 1.16: CHE parameter substitution.










CE Quantity
CHE Counterpart
Substitution




\(B_j^{\mathrm{CE}} = \sigma^2_\theta / (\sigma^2_\theta + \bar{s}_j^2/n_j)\)
\(B_j^{\mathrm{CHE}} = \sigma^2_\theta / (\sigma^2_\theta + (\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2)/n_j)\)
\(\bar{s}_j^2 \to \bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2\)


\(\rho_j^{\mathrm{CE}} = \sigma^2_\theta / (\bar{s}_j^2 + \sigma^2_\theta)\)
\(\rho_j^{\mathrm{CHE}} = \sigma^2_\theta / (\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2 + \sigma^2_\theta)\)
Same


\(\mathrm{DEFF}_j^{\mathrm{CE}} = 1 + (n_j - 1)\rho_j^{\mathrm{CE}}\)
\(\mathrm{DEFF}_j^{\mathrm{CHE}} = 1 + (n_j - 1)\rho_j^{\mathrm{CHE}}\)
Same






Since \(\omega^2 > 0\) strictly increases the denominator in the ICC, the ordering \(\rho_j^{\mathrm{CHE}} < \rho_j^{\mathrm{CE}}\), \(B_j^{\mathrm{CHE}} < B_j^{\mathrm{CE}}\), and \(\mathrm{DEFF}_j^{\mathrm{CHE}} < \mathrm{DEFF}_j^{\mathrm{CE}}\) holds ceteris paribus (i.e., holding \(\sigma^2_\theta\), \(\bar{s}_j^2\), and \(n_j\) fixed).

title: 1  Notation and Conventions
text:
1.8 Isomorphism Map
The isomorphism map \(\mathcal{T}\) sends each mathematical object in the survey framework to its meta-analytic counterpart (the full proof is given in Chapter 4, Theorem 1). The two core identifications are:
\[
\mathcal{T}: \quad \sigma^2_{e,j} \;\mapsto\; \bar{s}_j^2, \qquad \tilde{w}_{ij} \;\mapsto\; 1.
\]
Under \(\mathcal{T}\), the survey pseudo-log-likelihood and the meta-analytic log-likelihood differ by a constant \(c\) that does not depend on \(\boldsymbol{\phi}\):
\[
\ell^{(w)}_{\mathrm{surv}}(\boldsymbol{\phi}) = \ell_{\mathrm{meta}}(\boldsymbol{\phi}) + c.
\]
The constant \(c\) depends on \(\{\sigma^2_{e,j}\}\), \(\{\bar{s}_j^2\}\), \(\{\tilde{w}_{ij}\}\), \(\rho\), and \(\{n_j\}\), but not on \(\boldsymbol{\phi} = (\boldsymbol{\beta}^{\intercal}, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\intercal})^{\intercal}\).






NoteNote on \(\mathcal{T}\) and Condition (E4)



The isomorphism map \(\mathcal{T}\) has two core identifications, corresponding to conditions (E2) and (E3). Condition (E4) — that all predictors are study-level (\(\mathbf{x}_{ij} = \mathbf{x}_j\)) — is not part of the isomorphism. It is a DER simplification condition needed only for the closed-form between-direction result \(R_k = B\) in Theorem 3. When (E4) is relaxed, the isomorphism still holds under (E1), (E2), (E3), and (E5) alone. Outside the between direction, summaries such as \(\mathrm{DEFF}(1-\gamma_k B)\) should be treated as heuristic rather than theorem-level identities.

title: 1  Notation and Conventions
text:
1.9 CCC Transformation Notation
The Cholesky Calibration of Credible intervals (CCC) transforms uncorrected MCMC draws \(\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(s)}\) into corrected draws \(\boldsymbol{\phi}^{*(s)}\):



Table 1.18: CCC transformation notation.










Symbol
Definition
Meaning




\(\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}}\)
Posterior mean or mode
Center of the affine transformation


\(\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(s)}\)
\(s\)th uncorrected MCMC draw
Input to CCC


\(\boldsymbol{\phi}^{*(s)}\)
\(\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}} + \mathbf{L}_2\,\mathbf{L}_1^{-1}\,(\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(s)} - \hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}})\)
Corrected draw






The corrected draws satisfy \(\mathrm{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\phi}^{*(s)}) = \mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{sand}}\), recalibrating the posterior dispersion to match the sandwich variance.

title: 1  Notation and Conventions
text:
1.11 Typographic and Mathematical Conventions

Boldface: Vectors (\(\boldsymbol{\beta}\), \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\), \(\boldsymbol{\phi}\), \(\mathbf{y}_j\), \(\mathbf{r}_j\), \(\mathbf{s}_j\)) and matrices (\(\mathbf{H}\), \(\mathbf{M}\), \(\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{sand}}\), \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\), \(\mathbf{X}_j\)) are set in boldface.
Scalars: Scalar quantities (\(y_{ij}\), \(\theta_j\), \(\sigma^2_\theta\), \(\rho\), \(B_j\)) are set in italic.
Subscripts: The subscript convention \(j\) (cluster), \(i\) (within-cluster observation), \(k\) (coefficient), \(s\) (MCMC draw) is used consistently.
Superscripts: Framework labels appear as superscripts: \(\ell^{(w)}_{\mathrm{surv}}\), \(\ell_{\mathrm{meta}}\), \(\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{sand}}^{\mathrm{surv}}\), \(\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{sand}}^{\mathrm{meta}}\).
Transpose: The transpose of a vector or matrix is denoted \((\cdot)^{\intercal}\).
Cholesky: \(\mathrm{chol}(\mathbf{A})\) denotes the lower-triangular Cholesky factor of a positive-definite matrix \(\mathbf{A}\).
Gradient and Hessian: \(\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\phi}}\) denotes the gradient operator; \(\nabla^2_{\boldsymbol{\phi}}\) denotes the Hessian operator.
Positive definiteness: \(\mathbf{A} \succ 0\) means \(\mathbf{A}\) is positive definite.
Convergence in distribution: \(\overset{d}{\longrightarrow}\) denotes convergence in distribution as \(J \to \infty\).
End of proof: \(\blacksquare\) marks the end of a proof.






Lee, J. 2026. “Design Effect Ratios for Bayesian Survey Models: A Diagnostic Framework for Identifying Survey-Sensitive Parameters.” https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2603.07791.


Williams, Matthew R., and Terrance D. Savitsky. 2021. “Uncertainty Estimation for Pseudo-Bayesian Inference Under Complex Sampling.” International Statistical Review 89 (1): 72–107. https://doi.org/10.1111/insr.12376.

title: 2  Model Definitions
text:
2.1 The Correlated-Effects (CE) Meta-Analytic Model
This chapter provides the complete mathematical specification of every model that participates in the structural equivalence theory. We define the correlated-effects (CE) meta-analytic model, its survey counterpart, and the two extensions — the hierarchical-effects (HE) model and the correlated-and-hierarchical effects (CHE) model. For each model, we give the full likelihood, the relevant covariance structure, and (where applicable) the pseudo-likelihood and pseudo-posterior formulas. All notation follows the conventions established in Chapter 1.
Despite appearing as distinct models from different disciplines, the CE meta-analytic model and the survey pseudo-likelihood model share the same mathematical core: a normal Level 1 with cluster-specific error variances, a normal Level 2 with exchangeable random effects, and a compound-symmetric working covariance parameterized by \(\rho\). The structural equivalence theory of this book rests on this shared core. We define the meta-analytic and survey models in parallel, making the correspondence explicit at each step, before presenting the CHE extension that introduces within-study heterogeneity.

title: 2  Model Definitions
text:
2.1.1 Definition 1: CE Working Model






NoteDefinition 1 (Working Model — CE Meta-Analytic Model)



Let \(y_{ij}\) denote the \(i\)th effect-size estimate from study \(j\), with known sampling variance \(s_{ij}^2\), for \(i = 1, \ldots, n_j\) and \(j = 1, \ldots, J\). A two-level hierarchical working model for the vector \(\mathbf{y}_j = (y_{1j}, \ldots, y_{n_j j})^{\intercal}\) is:
Level 1 (within study): \[
y_{ij} \mid \theta_j, \boldsymbol{\beta} \sim \textsf{N}\!\left(\mathbf{x}_{ij}^{\intercal}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \theta_j,\; \bar{s}_j^2\right), \quad i = 1, \ldots, n_j,
\tag{2.1}\]
Level 2 (between studies): \[
\theta_j \mid \sigma^2_\theta \sim \textsf{N}(0,\, \sigma^2_\theta), \quad j = 1, \ldots, J,
\tag{2.2}\]
where \(\mathbf{x}_{ij} \in \mathbb{R}^p\) is a vector of predictors (moderators) that may vary within or across studies, \(\bar{s}_j^2 = n_j^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n_j} s_{ij}^2\) is the average within-study sampling variance, and \(\sigma^2_\theta > 0\) is the between-study heterogeneity variance.


Several features of this definition merit careful commentary.
Conditional independence and the role of \(\rho\). Level 1 in Equation 2.1 is the independence working model (\(\rho = 0\)): given the random effects \(\theta_j\) and fixed effects \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\), observations within study \(j\) are conditionally independent. When \(\rho > 0\), the operative Gaussian working model is the clusterwise multivariate normal model induced by Definition 2. In that case, Level 1 should be read as specifying the mean vector and marginal scale, while the within-study dependence is supplied by \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(\rho)\). Robust inference is obtained from the sandwich variance estimator rather than by claiming literal conditional independence when \(\rho>0\).
Broad CE definition. The CE model is defined by \(\omega^2 = 0\) (no within-study heterogeneity beyond sampling error), not by the covariate level. The predictor vector \(\mathbf{x}_{ij}\) may vary at the observation level within studies. The CE-vs-CHE distinction is about the variance structure (\(\omega^2 = 0\) vs \(\omega^2 > 0\)), not about whether covariates are study-level or observation-level (see Pustejovsky and Tipton 2022 for the working-model taxonomy).
Average sampling variance. The use of \(\bar{s}_j^2\) rather than \(s_{ij}^2\) at Level 1 reflects the compound-symmetry assumption: all effect sizes within a study are treated as having the same marginal sampling variance. When the individual variances \(s_{ij}^2\) are heterogeneous within study \(j\), this averaging introduces a working-model approximation. The sandwich variance protects against this misspecification.
Exchangeable random effects. Level 2 assumes that the study-specific true effects \(\theta_j\) are drawn from a common normal distribution centered at zero (after accounting for moderators), with between-study variance \(\sigma^2_\theta\).


2.1.2 Definition 2: CE Working Covariance






NoteDefinition 2 (CE Working Covariance)



Under the correlated effects assumption, the working covariance of \(\mathbf{y}_j\) given \(\theta_j\) takes the compound-symmetric form: \[
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(\rho) = \bar{s}_j^2\!\left[(1-\rho)\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \rho\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}\right],
\tag{2.3}\] where \(\rho \in [0, 1)\) is the assumed within-study sampling correlation and \(\mathbf{1}_{n_j} = (1, \ldots, 1)^{\intercal} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_j}\).


The compound-symmetric matrix \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(\rho)\) has a rich algebraic structure that is exploited throughout the theory. We record the essential identities here.

Eigenstructure
The compound-symmetric matrix \((1-\rho)\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \rho\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}\) has two distinct eigenvalues:

Eigenvalue \(1 + (n_j - 1)\rho\), with multiplicity 1, corresponding to the eigenvector \(\mathbf{1}_{n_j}/\sqrt{n_j}\).
Eigenvalue \((1-\rho)\), with multiplicity \(n_j - 1\), corresponding to any vector orthogonal to \(\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\).

Since \(\rho \in [0, 1)\), both eigenvalues are strictly positive, so \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(\rho)\) is positive definite.
Figure 2.1 visualizes this spectral decomposition as the within-study correlation \(\rho\) increases from zero toward one. In the heatmap panels, the off-diagonal entries of the compound-symmetric matrix grow with \(\rho\), while the lower panels show the between-study eigenvalue \(\lambda_{\mathrm{between}} = \bar{s}^2[1+(n-1)\rho]\) pulling away from the within-study eigenvalue \(\lambda_{\mathrm{within}} = \bar{s}^2(1-\rho)\). This geometric separation is the source of the design effect formulas derived in Part III.






Figure 2.1: Compound-symmetric working covariance across increasing within-study correlation. Heatmaps show the matrix itself, while the lower panels show the between-study eigenvalue \(\lambda_{\mathrm{between}}=\bar{s}^2[1+(n-1)\rho]\) separating from the within-study eigenvalue \(\lambda_{\mathrm{within}}=\bar{s}^2(1-\rho)\). This is the geometric source of the later DEFF formulas.





Inverse (Sherman–Morrison Formula)
The inverse of the compound-symmetric working covariance follows from the Sherman–Morrison formula applied to \(\mathbf{A} = \sigma^2(1-\rho)\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j}\) and \(\mathbf{u}\mathbf{v}^{\intercal} = \sigma^2\rho\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}\):
\[
\left[\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(\rho)\right]^{-1} = \frac{1}{\bar{s}_j^2(1-\rho)}\!\left[\mathbf{I}_{n_j} - \frac{\rho}{1 + (n_j - 1)\rho}\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}\right].
\tag{2.4}\]
Derivation. Write \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j = \alpha\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \gamma\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}\) with \(\alpha = \bar{s}_j^2(1-\rho)\) and \(\gamma = \bar{s}_j^2\rho\). By Sherman–Morrison:
\[
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{-1} = \alpha^{-1}\mathbf{I}_{n_j} - \frac{\alpha^{-1}\gamma\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}\alpha^{-1}}{1 + \gamma\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}(\alpha^{-1}\mathbf{I}_{n_j})\mathbf{1}_{n_j}} = \frac{1}{\alpha}\left[\mathbf{I}_{n_j} - \frac{\gamma/\alpha}{1 + n_j\gamma/\alpha}\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}\right].
\]
Substituting \(\gamma/\alpha = \rho/(1-\rho)\) and simplifying:
\[
\frac{\gamma/\alpha}{1 + n_j\gamma/\alpha} = \frac{\rho/(1-\rho)}{1 + n_j\rho/(1-\rho)} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho + n_j\rho} = \frac{\rho}{1 + (n_j-1)\rho}.
\]
This yields Equation 2.4.


Log-Determinant
Using the eigenvalue decomposition:
\[
\log\!\left|\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(\rho)\right| = (n_j - 1)\log\!\left[\bar{s}_j^2(1-\rho)\right] + \log\!\left[\bar{s}_j^2\!\left(1 + (n_j-1)\rho\right)\right].
\tag{2.5}\]
Derivation. The determinant of a matrix equals the product of its eigenvalues. The eigenvalues of \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(\rho)\) are \(\bar{s}_j^2(1-\rho)\) with multiplicity \(n_j - 1\) and \(\bar{s}_j^2[1 + (n_j-1)\rho]\) with multiplicity 1. Therefore:
\[
\left|\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(\rho)\right| = \left[\bar{s}_j^2(1-\rho)\right]^{n_j-1} \cdot \bar{s}_j^2\!\left[1 + (n_j-1)\rho\right].
\]
Taking logarithms gives Equation 2.5.


Special Cases

Independence (\(\rho = 0\)): \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(0) = \bar{s}_j^2\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j}\), and the inverse is \((\bar{s}_j^2)^{-1}\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j}\).
Perfect correlation (\(\rho \to 1\)): \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(\rho)\) approaches \(\bar{s}_j^2\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}\), which is rank-1 and not invertible. The theory requires \(\rho < 1\).



Marginal Covariance
Integrating out \(\theta_j\), the marginal covariance of \(\mathbf{y}_j\) is:
\[
\mathrm{Cov}(\mathbf{y}_j) = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(\rho) + \sigma^2_\theta\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal} = \bar{s}_j^2(1-\rho)\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \left(\bar{s}_j^2\rho + \sigma^2_\theta\right)\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}.
\tag{2.6}\]



2.1.3 Meta-Analytic Log-Likelihood
The log-likelihood for the full data \(\mathbf{y} = (\mathbf{y}_1^{\intercal}, \ldots, \mathbf{y}_J^{\intercal})^{\intercal}\), conditional on \(\boldsymbol{\phi} = (\boldsymbol{\beta}^{\intercal}, \boldsymbol{\theta}^{\intercal})^{\intercal}\) and treating \(\rho\) and \(\sigma^2_\theta\) as fixed/known, is:
\[
\ell_{\mathrm{meta}}(\boldsymbol{\phi}) = \sum_{j=1}^{J} \ell_j^{\mathrm{meta}}(\boldsymbol{\phi}),
\tag{2.7}\]
where the cluster-\(j\) contribution is:
\[
\ell_j^{\mathrm{meta}}(\boldsymbol{\phi}) = -\frac{n_j}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\log\!\left|\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(\rho)\right| - \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{r}_j^{\intercal}\!\left[\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work}}(\rho)\right]^{-1}\!\mathbf{r}_j,
\tag{2.8}\]
with the cluster-level residual vector \(\mathbf{r}_j = \mathbf{y}_j - \mathbf{X}_j\boldsymbol{\beta} - \theta_j\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\) and the design matrix \(\mathbf{X}_j\) being \(n_j \times p\) with row \(i\) equal to \(\mathbf{x}_{ij}^{\intercal}\).
Substituting the inverse (Equation 2.4) and log-determinant (Equation 2.5), the cluster-level log-likelihood can be written in scalar form as:
\[
\ell_j^{\mathrm{meta}}(\boldsymbol{\phi}) = -\frac{n_j}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{n_j-1}{2}\log[\bar{s}_j^2(1-\rho)] - \frac{1}{2}\log[\bar{s}_j^2(1+(n_j-1)\rho)] - \frac{1}{2\bar{s}_j^2(1-\rho)}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n_j} r_{ij}^2 - \frac{\rho}{1+(n_j-1)\rho}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n_j} r_{ij}\right)^2\right],
\tag{2.9}\]
where \(r_{ij} = y_{ij} - \mathbf{x}_{ij}^{\intercal}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \theta_j\).






ImportantKey Observation



The log-determinant terms in Equation 2.9 depend only on \(\bar{s}_j^2\), \(\rho\), and \(n_j\) — they do not depend on the parameter vector \(\boldsymbol{\phi}\). The entire \(\boldsymbol{\phi}\)-dependence of the log-likelihood is contained in the quadratic form. This observation is the engine of the isomorphism proof: any two likelihoods with the same quadratic form differ by a \(\boldsymbol{\phi}\)-free constant.

title: 2  Model Definitions
text:
2.2 The Bayesian Clustered Survey Model

2.2.1 Survey Model Specification: \(\rho = 0\) Case (Univariate)
Consider a clustered survey with \(J\) PSUs, where PSU \(j\) contributes \(n_j\) sampled units. Under a normal working model with independent within-cluster errors:
Level 1 (within cluster): \[
y_{ij} \mid \theta_j, \boldsymbol{\beta} \sim \textsf{N}\!\left(\mathbf{x}_{ij}^{\intercal}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \theta_j,\; \sigma^2_{e,j}\right), \quad i = 1, \ldots, n_j,
\tag{2.10}\]
Level 2 (between clusters): \[
\theta_j \mid \sigma^2_\theta \sim \textsf{N}(0,\, \sigma^2_\theta), \quad j = 1, \ldots, J.
\tag{2.11}\]
Here \(\sigma^2_{e,j}\) is the within-cluster error variance for cluster \(j\) (treated as known/conditioned upon), and \(\mathbf{x}_{ij} \in \mathbb{R}^p\) is a covariate vector that may vary at the observation level within clusters.






ImportantKey Correction: Cluster-Specific Error Variances



We use cluster-specific notation \(\sigma^2_{e,j}\) rather than a single common \(\sigma^2_e\). Exact isomorphism with the meta-analytic model requires the cluster-by-cluster identification \(\sigma^2_{e,j} = \bar{s}_j^2\). If a common \(\sigma^2_e\) is used while \(\bar{s}_j^2\) varies across studies, the quadratic forms in the two likelihoods differ by a \(\boldsymbol{\phi}\)-dependent term, breaking exact isomorphism. See Chapter 3, condition (E2).
When \(\bar{s}_j^2 = \bar{s}^2\) is genuinely common across all studies, a single \(\sigma^2_e = \bar{s}^2\) suffices.


The survey pseudo-log-likelihood with the \(\rho = 0\) working model is a weighted sum of univariate normal log-densities:
\[
\ell^{(w)}_{\mathrm{surv}}(\boldsymbol{\phi}) = \sum_{j=1}^{J}\sum_{i=1}^{n_j} \tilde{w}_{ij}\cdot\log f\!\left(y_{ij} \mid \mathbf{x}_{ij}^{\intercal}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \theta_j,\, \sigma^2_{e,j}\right),
\tag{2.12}\]
where \(f(y \mid \mu, \sigma^2) = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-1/2}\exp[-(y-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)]\) is the univariate normal density, and \(\tilde{w}_{ij} = N \cdot w_{ij}/\sum_{j'}\sum_{i'} w_{i'j'}\) are normalized weights summing to \(N\).
Expanding:
\[
\ell^{(w)}_{\mathrm{surv}}(\boldsymbol{\phi}) = \sum_{j=1}^{J}\sum_{i=1}^{n_j} \tilde{w}_{ij}\left[-\frac{1}{2}\log(2\pi\sigma^2_{e,j}) - \frac{(y_{ij} - \mathbf{x}_{ij}^{\intercal}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \theta_j)^2}{2\sigma^2_{e,j}}\right].
\tag{2.13}\]


2.2.2 Survey Model Specification: \(\rho > 0\) Case (Multivariate Clusterwise Normal)






ImportantCritical Clarification: Clusterwise Multivariate Formulation



When \(\rho > 0\), the survey pseudo-log-likelihood must be formulated as a clusterwise multivariate normal log-likelihood, not merely as a sum of weighted univariate log-densities. This distinction is essential because the compound-symmetric covariance introduces cross-terms \(r_{ij}r_{hj}\) (with \(i \neq h\)) in the quadratic form, which cannot be expressed as a sum of independent univariate contributions.
This formulation addresses a gap in the original formulation. Without the explicit clusterwise multivariate statement, the proof of Theorem 1 for \(\rho > 0\) is not self-contained.


When the within-cluster working covariance is compound-symmetric with correlation \(\rho > 0\), the survey model specifies the clusterwise multivariate normal distribution:
\[
\mathbf{y}_j \mid \theta_j, \boldsymbol{\beta} \sim \textsf{N}_{n_j}\!\left(\mathbf{X}_j\boldsymbol{\beta} + \theta_j\mathbf{1}_{n_j},\; \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work,surv}}(\rho)\right),
\tag{2.14}\]
where the within-cluster working covariance takes the compound-symmetric form:
\[
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work,surv}}(\rho) = \sigma^2_{e,j}\!\left[(1-\rho)\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \rho\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}\right].
\tag{2.15}\]
The Level 2 specification remains as in Equation 2.11.
The survey pseudo-log-likelihood under the \(\rho > 0\) working model is then:
\[
\ell^{(w)}_{\mathrm{surv}}(\boldsymbol{\phi}) = \sum_{j=1}^{J} \ell_j^{(w)}(\boldsymbol{\phi}),
\tag{2.16}\]
where each cluster-\(j\) contribution is the multivariate normal log-density:
\[
\ell_j^{(w)}(\boldsymbol{\phi}) = -\frac{n_j}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\log\!\left|\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work,surv}}(\rho)\right| - \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{r}_j^{\intercal}\!\left[\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{work,surv}}(\rho)\right]^{-1}\!\mathbf{r}_j,
\tag{2.17}\]
with \(\mathbf{r}_j = \mathbf{y}_j - \mathbf{X}_j\boldsymbol{\beta} - \theta_j\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\).






NoteNote on Weights in the \(\rho > 0\) Case



Under exactness condition (E3), \(\tilde{w}_{ij} = 1\) for all \((i,j)\). In this case, the weighted and unweighted likelihoods coincide, and the clusterwise formulation in Equation 2.17 is standard. When weights are non-unit, the appropriate formulation is a weighted multivariate normal pseudo-log-likelihood. The theory requires (E3) for exact isomorphism, so the unweighted clusterwise formulation suffices for the formal results.
For the \(\rho = 0\) case, the clusterwise and sum-of-univariate formulations are algebraically equivalent (no cross-terms), so the distinction is immaterial.



Connecting the Two Cases
When \(\rho = 0\), the compound-symmetric covariance reduces to \(\sigma^2_{e,j}\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j}\), and the clusterwise multivariate formulation (Equation 2.17) reduces to the sum of univariate contributions (Equation 2.13) with \(\tilde{w}_{ij} = 1\). The two cases form a unified family parameterized by \(\rho \in [0, 1)\).



2.2.3 Survey Pseudo-Posterior
The survey pseudo-posterior combines the pseudo-log-likelihood with the prior:
\[
\pi^*_{\mathrm{surv}}(\boldsymbol{\phi}, \sigma^2_\theta \mid \mathbf{y}) \propto \exp\!\left[\ell^{(w)}_{\mathrm{surv}}(\boldsymbol{\phi})\right] \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{J} \textsf{N}(\theta_j \mid 0, \sigma^2_\theta) \cdot p(\boldsymbol{\beta})\,p(\sigma^2_\theta).
\tag{2.18}\]






NoteConvention: Treatment of \(\rho\)



Throughout the theory, the working correlation \(\rho\) is treated as fixed (a sensitivity parameter), not as an estimated parameter. If \(\rho\) were included in \(\boldsymbol{\phi}\) and estimated, the constant \(c\) in the isomorphism could inherit \(\rho\)-dependence, potentially invalidating the likelihood equivalence. In meta-analytic practice, \(\rho\) is typically set to a fixed value (e.g., 0.8) or varied in a sensitivity analysis.

title: 2  Model Definitions
text:
2.3 The Meta-Analytic Posterior
The meta-analytic working-model posterior is:
\[
\pi_{\mathrm{meta}}(\boldsymbol{\phi}, \sigma^2_\theta \mid \mathbf{y}) \propto \exp\!\left[\ell_{\mathrm{meta}}(\boldsymbol{\phi})\right] \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{J} \textsf{N}(\theta_j \mid 0, \sigma^2_\theta) \cdot p(\boldsymbol{\beta})\,p(\sigma^2_\theta).
\tag{2.19}\]
When the two likelihoods satisfy \(\ell^{(w)}_{\mathrm{surv}}(\boldsymbol{\phi}) = \ell_{\mathrm{meta}}(\boldsymbol{\phi}) + c\) with \(c\) independent of \(\boldsymbol{\phi}\) and \(\sigma^2_\theta\), the factor \(e^c\) cancels between numerator and denominator in Bayes’ theorem, yielding posterior equivalence (see Corollary 1 in the main theory).

title: 2  Model Definitions
text:
2.4 The Within-Cluster-Covariate (E4-Relaxed CE) Extension
Earlier drafts used the label “HE” for the case where the CE variance structure is retained (\(\omega^2 = 0\)) but predictors vary within study. To avoid collision with the literature’s variance-model taxonomy, this book now treats that case as an E4-relaxed CE mean-structure extension, not as a distinct variance model. The variance structure remains CE; only the design matrix is generalized.






NoteDefinition (E4-Relaxed CE Working Model)



The E4-relaxed CE extension keeps the CE variance model (\(\omega^2 = 0\)) and allows the rows of \(\mathbf{X}_j\) to vary within study:
Level 1 (within study): \[
y_{ij} \mid \theta_j, \boldsymbol{\beta} \sim \textsf{N}\!\left(\mathbf{x}_{ij}^{\intercal}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \theta_j,\; \bar{s}_j^2\right), \quad i = 1, \ldots, n_j,
\tag{2.20}\]
Level 2 (between studies): \[
\theta_j \mid \sigma^2_\theta \sim \textsf{N}(0,\, \sigma^2_\theta), \quad j = 1, \ldots, J.
\tag{2.21}\]
The working covariance remains compound-symmetric as in Definition 2 (Equation 2.3). The design matrix \(\mathbf{X}_j\) is now \(n_j \times p\) with row \(i\) equal to \(\mathbf{x}_{ij}^{\intercal}\), which may differ across rows within a study.


Relationship to CE. The CE model is defined by \(\omega^2 = 0\), not by the covariate level. The E4-relaxed CE extension is therefore not a new variance model; it is simply the broad CE mean structure with condition (E4) removed.
Isomorphism under the E4-relaxed CE extension. Observation-level covariates enter the likelihood only through the residual vector \(\mathbf{r}_j = \mathbf{y}_j - \mathbf{X}_j\boldsymbol{\beta} - \theta_j\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\), and the log-determinant term is independent of the mean structure. Therefore, if the survey model and the meta-analytic model use the same \(\mathbf{X}_j\), Theorems 1–2 and Corollaries 1–2 hold under (E1), (E2), (E3), and (E5) alone — condition (E4) is not needed. See Chapter 3 for the precise statement.
DER under the E4-relaxed CE extension. While the isomorphism is unaffected by within-study-varying covariates, the simple between-direction DER formula is not. A useful balanced-design summary is \[
\mathrm{DER}_{\beta_k}^{\mathrm{E4-relaxed}} \approx \mathrm{DEFF} \cdot (1 - \gamma_k B),
\tag{2.22}\] where \(\gamma_k = \mathrm{Var}_{\mathrm{between}}(\mathbf{x}_{jk}) / \mathrm{Var}_{\mathrm{total}}(\mathbf{x}_{ijk})\) is a heuristic descriptor of how much coefficient \(k\) is identified by between-study variation. This approximation is exact for the intercept and study-level covariates (the between direction), but not for general within-cluster covariates. For purely within-cluster covariates at \(\rho_{\mathrm{work}} = 0\), the relevant ratio can be closer to \(1-\rho_{\mathrm{true}}\) than to the classical scalar DEFF; see Section 11.3 for details.

title: 2  Model Definitions
text:
2.5 The Correlated-and-Hierarchical Effects (CHE) Model
The CHE model, formalized by Pustejovsky and Tipton (2022), adds a within-study heterogeneity component to the CE model.






NoteDefinition (CHE Working Model)



The CHE model is a three-level hierarchy:
Level 1 (sampling error): \[
y_{ij} = \mathbf{x}_{ij}^{\intercal}\boldsymbol{\beta} + u_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij}, \quad \varepsilon_{ij} \sim \textsf{N}(0,\, s_{ij}^2),
\tag{2.23}\]
Level 2 (within-study random effect): \[
u_{ij} = \theta_j + e_{ij}, \quad e_{ij} \sim \textsf{N}(0,\, \omega^2),
\tag{2.24}\]
Level 3 (between-study random effect): \[
\theta_j \sim \textsf{N}(0,\, \sigma^2_\theta),
\tag{2.25}\]
where \(\omega^2 \geq 0\) is the within-study heterogeneity variance (the variance of true effect sizes within a study, beyond sampling error) and \(\sigma^2_\theta > 0\) is the between-study heterogeneity variance.



2.5.1 CHE Working Covariance
Conditional on \(\theta_j\) and applying the common-variance simplification \(s_{ij}^2 = \bar{s}_j^2\):
\[
\mathrm{Cov}(\mathbf{y}_j \mid \theta_j) = (\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2)\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j}.
\tag{2.26}\]
Integrating out \(\theta_j\):
\[
\mathrm{Cov}(\mathbf{y}_j) = (\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2)\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \sigma^2_\theta\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}.
\tag{2.27}\]
This has the compound-symmetric form:
\[
\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j^{\mathrm{CHE}} = (\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2 + \sigma^2_\theta)\left[(1 - \rho_j^{\mathrm{CHE}})\,\mathbf{I}_{n_j} + \rho_j^{\mathrm{CHE}}\,\mathbf{1}_{n_j}\mathbf{1}_{n_j}^{\intercal}\right],
\tag{2.28}\]
where the CHE-implied correlation (study-specific via \(\bar{s}_j^2\)) is:
\[
\rho_j^{\mathrm{CHE}} = \frac{\sigma^2_\theta}{\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2 + \sigma^2_\theta}.
\tag{2.29}\]






ImportantKey Observation: CHE as CE with Parameter Substitution



At the conditional likelihood level (conditioning on \(\theta_j\)), the CHE model is identical in form to the CE model with the substitution: \[
\bar{s}_j^2 \;\mapsto\; \bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2.
\] This substitution is the rigorous mechanism by which CE results transfer to the CHE setting. In particular, the CE isomorphism, sandwich equivalence, posterior equivalence, and CCC equivalence all carry over under the modified exactness condition (E2’): \(\sigma^2_{e,\mathrm{total},j} = \bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2\).
At the marginal level (after integrating out \(\theta_j\)), the total scale factor is \(\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2 + \sigma^2_\theta\) (not \(\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2\) as was incorrectly stated in the original formulation; the corrected expression is used throughout).








NoteCaveats on the CHE Substitution



The substitution \(\bar{s}_j^2 \mapsto \bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2\) yields exact results only when:

\(\omega^2\) is fixed and known (or conditioned upon), not estimated as part of \(\boldsymbol{\phi}\).
\(\omega^2\) is common across studies (not study-specific).
The survey-side model is enlarged in exactly the same way, with the same prior and identification map.

When \(\omega^2\) is estimated, the posterior over the enlarged parameter space \((\boldsymbol{\phi}, \omega^2, \sigma^2_\theta)\) requires the survey model to be enlarged correspondingly. The claim that “all CE results carry over” is exact only under the fixed-known/common-\(\omega^2\) setup.




2.5.2 Reduced Design Sensitivity Under CHE
Since \(\omega^2 > 0\) increases the denominator of the ICC:
\[
\rho_j^{\mathrm{CHE}} = \frac{\sigma^2_\theta}{\bar{s}_j^2 + \omega^2 + \sigma^2_\theta} < \frac{\sigma^2_\theta}{\bar{s}_j^2 + \sigma^2_\theta} = \rho_j^{\mathrm{CE}},
\]
which implies (ceteris paribus, holding \(\sigma^2_\theta\), \(\bar{s}_j^2\), and \(n_j\) fixed):

\(\mathrm{DEFF}^{\mathrm{CHE}} < \mathrm{DEFF}^{\mathrm{CE}}\)
\(B^{\mathrm{CHE}} < B^{\mathrm{CE}}\)
\(\mathrm{DER}_\mu^{\mathrm{CHE}} < \mathrm{DER}_\mu^{\mathrm{CE}}\)

Practical implication. Misspecifying a CE model when the true data-generating process is CHE will over-attribute design sensitivity: the DER will appear larger than it should be, and the CCC correction will be overly aggressive.






NoteCaveat on Ceteris Paribus



The ordering \(\mathrm{DEFF}^{\mathrm{CHE}} < \mathrm{DEFF}^{\mathrm{CE}}\) is a parameter-level inequality holding the other quantities (\(\sigma^2_\theta\), \(\bar{s}_j^2\), \(n_j\)) fixed. It is not automatically a statement about fitted values after refitting CE and CHE models to the same dataset, because the estimate of \(\sigma^2_\theta\) typically changes between the two fits (part of \(\sigma^2_\theta\) from CE is absorbed into \(\omega^2\) under CHE).
