title: 12  Interpretive Issues and Practical Implications
text:
12.5 Recommendations for Practice
Based on the theoretical analysis and the TSL15 illustration, we offer the following recommendations for practitioners applying the DER/CCC framework to Bayesian meta-analysis with dependent effect sizes.

12.5.1 Recommendation 1: Always report DER alongside meta-analytic output
The DER provides a per-parameter diagnostic for working-model misspecification that is unavailable from standard meta-analytic output. The DER variant taxonomy (Definition 5b, Section 7.1.2.1) identifies four distinct quantities; at minimum, report:

DER\(_\mu^{\mathrm{marg}}\) (variant (iii)): the Bayesian marginal DER for the overall mean. This is the primary CCC decision criterion for \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) (see Definition 3a, Section 5.4.2).
DER\(_\mu^{\mathrm{cond}}\) (variant (i)): the conditional DER \(\mathrm{DEFF}(1-B)\) for the overall mean. This is the theoretical decomposition diagnostic from Theorem 3(a) and the conservation law (Theorem 3(c)).
Mean and range of DER\(_\theta^{\mathrm{cond}}\) (variant (i)): the conditional design-effect diagnostic for study-specific effects. This is the only DER variant available for \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\).
DER\(_{\beta_k}^{\mathrm{marg}}\) (variant (iii)): for each meta-regression coefficient (if applicable).
DER\(_\mu^{\mathrm{CR2}}\) (variant (iv), optional): the CR2-based DER as an external validation benchmark.

These diagnostics should be reported alongside standard output (forest plot, posterior summaries, heterogeneity estimates) to give readers a sense of how sensitive the posterior is to the within-study working correlation assumption. The marginal DER is the primary CCC decision criterion for \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\), replacing the CR2-based DER.


12.5.2 Recommendation 2: Interpret the DER taxonomy correctly
The conditional DER\(_\mu^{\mathrm{cond}} = \mathrm{DEFF}(1-B)\) is a design-based diagnostic that answers: “How much does the within-study design effect inflate (or deflate) the posterior variance of \(\mu\), after accounting for hierarchical shrinkage?” It does not serve as a CCC decision criterion. The marginal DER \(\mathrm{DER}_\mu^{\mathrm{marg}}\) from the Bayesian marginal sandwich (Definition 5b(iii)) is the CCC decision quantity.
The three DER quantities for \(\mu\) answer different questions:

\(\mathrm{DER}_\mu^{\mathrm{cond}} = \mathrm{DEFF}(1-B)\) (conditional): “What is the within-study design effect on \(\mu\) conditional on \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\)?” Answers the theoretical decomposition question (Theorem 3). Typically \(\ll 1\) for \(\mu\) when \(B\) is large.
\(\mathrm{DER}_\mu^{\mathrm{marg}}\) (Bayesian marginal): “Is the posterior for \(\mu\) well-calibrated relative to the marginal sandwich?” This is the CCC decision criterion. Values \(> 1\) trigger CCC widening; values \(< 1\) indicate the posterior is already conservative.
\(B + \mathrm{DEFF}(1-B)\) (total variance ratio): “Is the posterior for \(\mu\) correctly calibrated in the frequentist sense?” Always \(\geq \max(\mathrm{DER}_\mu^{\mathrm{marg}}, 1)\) and captures the full model-plus-design inflation.

Specifically:

\(\mathrm{DER}_\mu^{\mathrm{marg}} > 1\): The posterior for \(\mu\) is under-dispersed relative to the marginal sandwich target. Apply CCC to widen.
\(\mathrm{DER}_\mu^{\mathrm{marg}} \in [0.9, 1.0]\): The posterior is approximately calibrated at the marginal level. No CCC needed.
\(\mathrm{DER}_\mu^{\mathrm{marg}} < 0.9\): The posterior is already wider than the marginal sandwich target. Do not apply automatic CCC narrowing. See Recommendation 5.



12.5.3 Recommendation 3: Widen forest plot intervals when DER\(_\theta > 1\)
In virtually all meta-analytic applications with dependent effect sizes, DER\(_\theta^{\mathrm{cond}} > 1\) — often substantially so (DER\(_\theta \approx 5\) in the TSL15 example). This means the model-based posterior credible intervals for study-specific effects \(\theta_j\) are too narrow.
Action. Apply the CCC correction to the study-specific effect posteriors. The corrected 95% credible interval for \(\theta_j\) will be approximately \(\sqrt{\mathrm{DER}_j^{\mathrm{cond}}}\) times wider than the uncorrected interval. For the TSL15 data, this means widening by a factor of approximately \(\sqrt{5.1} \approx 2.3\).
Note on conditional vs. marginal correction. The factor \(\sqrt{\mathrm{DER}_j^{\mathrm{cond}}}\) is the conditional-on-\(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) correction. Forest plots typically display marginal posterior intervals for \(\theta_j\) (integrating over \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)). For large \(J\), the coupling factor \(\kappa_j(J) \approx 1\) and the marginal DER is approximately equal to the conditional DER, so the distinction is negligible. For small \(J\) (e.g., \(J < 30\)), the balanced-design marginal factor should be treated as a scalar sensitivity adjustment unless a full marginal random-effect sandwich has been explicitly constructed; see Definition 8 for the coupling factor formula.
Note on balance assumption. The closed-form formula \(\kappa_j(J) = 1 - [J(1-B_j) + B_j]^{-1}\) stated in Definition 8 is exact in balanced designs (\(n_j = n\), \(\bar{s}_j^2 = \bar{s}^2\) for all \(j\)). In unbalanced designs, the marginal posterior variance of \(\theta_j\) depends on the full precision schedule \(\{a_h = n_h/\bar{s}_h^2\}_{h=1}^J\), and \(\kappa_j(J)\) is an approximation (see the scope note at Definition 8). For highly unbalanced meta-analyses, the conditional DER \(B_j \cdot \mathrm{DEFF}_j\) remains exact and may be the more reliable correction target.
Forest plots displaying uncorrected credible intervals for study-specific effects will systematically overstate the precision of individual study estimates, potentially leading to misleading visual impressions of heterogeneity and study-level significance.


12.5.4 Recommendation 4: Use \(N_{\mathrm{eff}}\) as an interpretive aid
The design-effective sample size \[
N_{\mathrm{eff}}^{\mathrm{design}}(\beta_k) = \frac{N}{\mathrm{DER}_{\beta_k}}
\] provides an intuitive summary of the information content in the meta-analytic sample. Report \(N_{\mathrm{eff}}\) alongside \(N\) to communicate the practical impact of within-study dependence.
For the TSL15 data, the effective sample size depends on which DER variant is used:



Table 12.3: Design-effective sample size under different DER variants for TSL15.











DER Variant
\(\mathrm{DER}_\mu\)
\(N_{\mathrm{eff}}^{\mathrm{design}}(\mu)\)
Interpretation




Conditional (i)
0.096
12,479
Spuriously large (\(\hat{\theta}_j\) absorbs variation)


Profile (ii)
0.95
1,261
Near-full efficiency (conditions on \(\tau^2\))


Marginal (iii)
1.21
990

CR2-based (iv)
0.86
1,393
3-level model estimate






The recommended \(N_{\mathrm{eff}}\) uses the marginal DER: \(N_{\mathrm{eff}}^{\mathrm{design}}(\mu) = 1{,}198/1.21 \approx 990\) effective observations. This is the most conservative (smallest) among the non-conditional variants, reflecting the full uncertainty including \(\tau^2\) estimation. For study-specific effects, \(N_{\mathrm{eff}}\) is much smaller due to the large \(\mathrm{DER}_\theta^{\mathrm{cond}} \approx 5.1\).


12.5.5 Recommendation 5: CCC decision logic for \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) via the DER taxonomy
The CCC decision for \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) depends critically on which sandwich variant defines the DER. The DER variant taxonomy (Definition 5b, Section 7.1.2.1) distinguishes four quantities; the following decision logic uses the Bayesian marginal DER (\(\mathrm{DER}_k^{\mathrm{marg}}\), variant (iii)) as the primary criterion.






ImportantAlgorithm: CCC Decision Flowchart for \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)



Input: MCMC draws \(\{\boldsymbol{\beta}^{(s)}\}_{s=1}^S\), posterior covariance \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathrm{MCMC}}\), data \(\{(\bar{y}_j, \mathbf{\bar{x}}_j, \bar{s}_j^2, n_j)\}_{j=1}^J\).
Output: Decision (apply CCC / skip CCC) and, if applied, corrected draws \(\{\boldsymbol{\beta}^{*(s)}\}\).
Step 1. Compute the Bayesian marginal sandwich \(\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{sand}}^{\mathrm{marg}}\) (Definition 3a, Section 5.4.2) when the coefficient of interest lies in the intercept / study-level-covariate setting covered by the cluster-mean closed form. For coefficients with substantial within-study-varying covariate structure, use the full multivariate marginal sandwich if available; otherwise use the profile or CR2 variant as the operational benchmark.
Step 2. Compute \(\mathrm{DER}_k^{\mathrm{marg}} = [\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{sand}}^{\mathrm{marg}}]_{kk} / [\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathrm{MCMC}}]_{kk}\) for each \(\beta_k\).
Step 3. Apply the classification:

\(\mathrm{DER}_k^{\mathrm{marg}} > 1\): Apply CCC to widen the posterior. The posterior is under-dispersed relative to the marginal sandwich target.
\(\mathrm{DER}_k^{\mathrm{marg}} \in [0.9, 1.0]\): No CCC needed. The posterior is approximately calibrated.
\(\mathrm{DER}_k^{\mathrm{marg}} < 0.9\): Do not apply automatic CCC narrowing. Report the DER as a diagnostic. See below.

Step 4 (validation). Compute \(\mathrm{DER}_k^{\mathrm{CR2}}\) (variant (iv)) as an external check on the marginal sandwich. If \(|\mathrm{DER}_k^{\mathrm{marg}} - \mathrm{DER}_k^{\mathrm{CR2}}| / \mathrm{DER}_k^{\mathrm{CR2}} > 0.25\), investigate the model-level discrepancy (e.g., 2-level vs. 3-level specification).


Why the Bayesian marginal DER is the primary criterion. In the intercept / study-level-covariate setting covered by Definition 3a, the marginal sandwich captures the full between-study variation in the residuals \(r_j = \bar{y}_j - \mathbf{\bar{x}}_j^{\intercal}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\), including the \(\tau^2\) component that the conditional sandwich misses. This makes \(\mathrm{DER}_k^{\mathrm{marg}}\) the most informative single diagnostic for whether the posterior is well-calibrated at the marginal level. For the TSL15 data, \(\mathrm{DER}_\mu^{\mathrm{marg}} = 1.21 > 1\), and the \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)-block CCC is applied (widening the posterior).
When marginal DER\(_\mu < 1\). The CCC correction would narrow the posterior credible interval for \(\mu\). This can occur when the (E2) specification already widens the posterior beyond the marginal sandwich target. In this case:

Report DER\(_\mu^{\mathrm{marg}}\) as a diagnostic quantity — it shows that the posterior is already wider than the marginal sandwich target.
Do not apply automatic CCC narrowing to \(\mu\) when DER\(_\mu^{\mathrm{marg}} < 1\).
Report the total variance ratio \(B + \mathrm{DEFF}(1-B)\) as a complementary diagnostic from the model-based frequentist perspective.
Use the uncorrected posterior interval for \(\mu\) as the default when DER\(_\mu^{\mathrm{marg}} < 1\), since narrowing would worsen frequentist coverage.

Why bracketing fails. The total model-based frequentist variance ratio is \(B + \mathrm{DEFF}(1-B)\), which can exceed 1 even when \(\mathrm{DER}_\mu^{\mathrm{marg}} < 1\). In such cases, both the uncorrected posterior interval and the CCC-corrected interval are narrower than the true frequentist interval.
Role of each DER variant in interpretation. The following table summarizes which DER variant answers which question:



Table 12.4: Role of each DER variant.










DER Variant
Notation
Primary Use




(i) Conditional
\(\mathrm{DER}_k^{\mathrm{thm,cond}}\) (or empirical conditional plug-in, if explicitly stated)
Theorem 3 decomposition; conservation law; \(\boldsymbol{\theta}\)-block CCC


(ii) Profile
\(\mathrm{DER}_k^{\mathrm{profile}}\)
Approximate match to CR2; conditions on \(\tau^2\)


(iii) Marginal
\(\mathrm{DER}_k^{\mathrm{marg}}\)
Primary CCC decision criterion for \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\)


(iv) CR2-based
\(\mathrm{DER}_k^{\mathrm{CR2}}\)
External validation benchmark






Note on the conditional DER. Two distinct “conditional DER” values arise for \(\mu\) and must not be conflated:

Empirical plug-in ratio: \([\mathbf{V}_{\mathrm{sand}}^{\mathrm{cond}}]_{\mu\mu}/[\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\mathrm{MCMC}}]_{\mu\mu} \approx 0.096\) (TSL15). This is the actual full-joint sandwich ratio at the MAP, where conditional residuals are near zero.
Theoretical formula: \(\mathrm{DER}_\mu^{\mathrm{thm,cond}} = \mathrm{DEFF}(1-B) \approx 5.62 \times 0.19 \approx 1.07\). This is the design-based decomposition from Theorem 3(a) using population-level quantities.

The empirical plug-in value (\(0.096\)) is typically far below 1 and should not be used for the CCC decision on \(\mu\). The theorem-level conditional value (\(1.07\)) is meaningful as the design-based diagnostic and for the conservation law (Theorem 3(c)). The marginal DER (\(\mathrm{DER}_\mu^{\mathrm{marg}} = 1.21\)) is the CCC decision criterion in the scope covered by Definition 3a. See Table 12.1 for the complete five-variant comparison.


12.5.6 Recommendation 6: The DER tier classification (T0–T4)
To aid interpretation, we restate the DER classification from Chapter 7, with a focus on the overall mean \(\mu\):



Table 12.5: DER tier classification (Lee 2026). T0–T4 thresholds are 0.95/1.05/2.0/4.0; see also Table 7.2 for the general classification.











Tier
DER Range
Label
Action




T0
\(< 0.95\)
Over-dispersed
Posterior wider than design warrants; do not narrow automatically; inspect model


T1
\([0.95, 1.05]\)
Calibrated
Working model adequate; no correction needed


T2
\((1.05, 2.0]\)
Mild
Report DER; CCC optional


T3
\((2.0, 4.0]\)
Moderate
Apply CCC; report both intervals


T4
\(> 4.0\)
Severe
CCC essential; consider model respecification






For study-specific effects \(\theta_j\), DER values in tiers T3–T4 are common in meta-analysis with dependent effect sizes. The CCC correction should be applied routinely for forest plot intervals.
Final remark. The DER/CCC framework does not replace careful model building and sensitivity analysis. Rather, it provides a quantitative diagnostic for a specific form of misspecification (within-study working correlation) that is inherent in all correlated-effects meta-analysis. By reporting DER alongside standard meta-analytic output, practitioners enable readers to assess the sensitivity of conclusions to the assumed dependence structure — a transparency that is currently absent from the meta-analytic reporting literature.




Lee, J. 2026. “Design Effect Ratios for Bayesian Survey Models: A Diagnostic Framework for Identifying Survey-Sensitive Parameters.” https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2603.07791.


Tanner-Smith, Emily E., and Mark W. Lipsey. 2015. “Brief Alcohol Interventions for Adolescents and Young Adults: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.” Journal of Substance Abuse Treatment 51: 1–18. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jsat.2014.09.001.

crumbs: 12  Interpretive Issues and Practical Implications

title: 13  Discussion and Conclusions
text:
13.1 Discussion: What the Equivalence Means
The preceding chapters have established a precise mathematical relationship between two inferential traditions that developed largely in isolation. Bayesian meta-analysis and Bayesian survey inference, when formulated under five exactness conditions, produce identical posterior distributions, identical sandwich variance estimators, and identical corrected credible intervals. This final chapter reflects on what that equivalence means, what it enables in practice, where it breaks down, and where the program might go next.
The central result of this book is not an approximation, an analogy, or a heuristic correspondence. Under conditions (E1)–(E3) and (E5), the meta-analytic log-likelihood and the survey pseudo-log-likelihood differ by a constant that depends on no model parameters (Theorem 1, Chapter 4). The constructive map \(\mathcal{T}\) that sends cluster-specific sampling variances \(\sigma^2_{e,j}\) to their within-cluster means \(\bar{s}^2_j\) and survey weights \(\tilde{w}_{ij}\) to unity is not a convenient simplification — it is an exact algebraic identification. The two frameworks are the same mathematical object written in different notation.
This recognition carries an immediate and somewhat startling implication: any researcher who fits a compound-exchangeable or correlated-hierarchical-effects meta-analysis in Stan is already computing the survey pseudo-posterior. The inferential machinery of design-based survey statistics — sandwich variance estimation, design effects, the Cholesky Calibration Correction — applies to those posterior draws whether or not the analyst invokes it. The equivalence exists in the mathematics; the theorems in this book merely make it visible.
Several conceptual clarifications follow. First, the distinction between conditional and marginal design effect ratios, developed in Section 12.1 and formalized in Theorem 3, had no precedent in the meta-analytic literature. The decomposition \(\mathrm{DER}^{\mathrm{cond}}_{\theta_j} = B_j \cdot \mathrm{DEFF}_j\), which separates the Bayesian shrinkage factor \(B_j\) from the classical design effect, reveals that random-effects shrinkage and survey weighting interact at the level of individual cluster parameters — a phenomenon that neither tradition had reason to articulate on its own. Second, the conservation law \(\mathrm{DER}_\mu + \overline{\mathrm{DER}}^{\,\mathrm{cond}}_\theta = \mathrm{DEFF}\) (Section 8.1; see Figure 8.1 for the geometric trade-off) shows that the total design effect is partitioned, not dissipated, across the parameter hierarchy. A near-unity \(\mathrm{DER}_\mu\) does not mean the design effect has vanished; it means the effect has been absorbed into the cluster-level parameters.
The practical consequence is direct. A meta-analyst who computes DER values for each parameter in the model can now determine exactly which credible intervals are trustworthy and which require sandwich-based correction. The Tanner-Smith and Lipsey (2015) empirical application illustrates this vividly: the overall mean \(\mu\) sits comfortably in the calibrated tier (\(\mathrm{DER}_\mu \approx 1.01\), T1), while the average cluster-level DER falls in the severe tier (\(\overline{\mathrm{DER}}_\theta \approx 5.09\), T4). Without the equivalence framework, the analyst has no principled basis for knowing which parameters to trust and which to correct. With it, the answer is arithmetic.

title: 13  Discussion and Conclusions
text:
13.2 Implications for Practice
The equivalence results speak to three overlapping audiences, each of which gains something distinct.
For meta-analysts, the most immediate payoff is diagnostic. The DER, computed from posterior draws and the sandwich variance estimator as described in Section 7.1, provides a scalar summary of model–design discrepancy for every parameter in the model. The tier classification from Lee (2026b) — T0 through T4, with boundaries at 0.95, 1.05, 2.0, and 4.0 — gives the analyst a standardized severity scale for each parameter. The CCC (Section 6.2) provides a post-MCMC correction that recalibrates posterior draws via a Cholesky transformation, requiring only matrix algebra on quantities already available from the Stan fit. No refitting is needed. The workflow is: fit once, diagnose with DER, correct where needed. In the Tanner-Smith and Lipsey (2015) data, the mean \(\mathrm{DER}_\theta = 5.09\) (tier T4) implies that standard forest plot intervals should be widened by a factor of approximately \(\sqrt{5.09} \approx 2.3\) — a correction that is invisible to conventional meta-analytic output.
For survey statisticians, the equivalence provides a mirror image. The conservation law, \(\mathrm{DER}_\mu + \overline{\mathrm{DER}}^{\,\mathrm{cond}}_\theta = \mathrm{DEFF}\), offers a decomposition of the classical design effect into components associated with different levels of the parameter hierarchy — a partition that has no natural expression within the purely design-based tradition. The three-step gradient observed in the Tanner-Smith and Lipsey (2015) application (within-study moderators at \(\sim 0.97\), between-study moderators at \(\sim 1.11\), control covariates at \(\sim 2.8\)–\(3.0\)) demonstrates that design effects are not uniform across parameters but vary systematically with the covariate’s position relative to the clustering structure. Survey practitioners working with hierarchical Bayesian models can use these results to anticipate which parameters will be most sensitive to design misspecification.
For methodologists, the deeper contribution is the demonstration that Calibrated Bayes (Little 2004, 2012) — the principle that Bayesian credible intervals should be evaluated against their design-based frequentist coverage — can be operationalized at the individual parameter level through the compute–classify–correct workflow. The DER is, in effect, a parameter-specific calibration diagnostic. When \(\mathrm{DER}_{\beta_k} \approx 1\), the model-based and design-based intervals agree; the posterior is already calibrated. When \(\mathrm{DER}_{\beta_k} \gg 1\), the model-based interval is too narrow; calibration requires the sandwich correction. The connection to Rubin (1984) is direct: the routine frequentist evaluation of Bayesian procedures that Rubin advocated is now embedded in the inferential pipeline at the individual-parameter level, at negligible computational cost relative to the MCMC sampling itself.

title: 13  Discussion and Conclusions
text:
13.3 Limitations
Six limitations of the present work deserve explicit acknowledgment.
First, the equivalence between meta-analytic and survey frameworks is exact only under compound exchangeability. The extension to correlated hierarchical effects (CHE), discussed in Section 10.2, is conditional on the within-study heterogeneity variance \(\omega^2\): the log-likelihoods coincide at each fixed \(\omega^2\), but the marginal likelihood over \(\omega^2\) introduces a term that depends on the framework-specific parameterization. Practical implications are minor when \(\omega^2\) is well identified, but the analyst should be aware that the CHE equivalence is not fully unconditional.
Second, exactness condition (E2) requires that the sampling variances \(\bar{s}_j^2\) be known. In practice, these are estimated from primary-study data and carry estimation uncertainty. When the estimated variances are substituted for the true values, the isomorphism holds approximately, with the approximation quality depending on the primary-study sample sizes. For meta-analyses with small primary studies, the (E2) violation may be consequential.
Third, condition (E5) restricts the framework to Gaussian Level 1 errors. This covers continuous effect sizes (standardized mean differences, Fisher-\(z\) correlations, log-odds ratios) under the usual normal approximation, but excludes bounded, count, and binary outcomes. The extension to non-Gaussian likelihoods requires an estimating-equation approach, as demonstrated for the hurdle beta-binomial model in Lee (2026a), but the isomorphism theory of this book does not apply directly.
Fourth, the empirical illustration relies on a single dataset (Tanner-Smith and Lipsey 2015) with \(J = 117\) studies and \(N = 1{,}198\) effect sizes. While the theoretical results are exact under the stated conditions and do not require empirical validation, the practical performance of the DER/CCC pipeline — including the conservation gap (8.7%) and the DER gradient across covariate types — has been documented in only one dataset. Generalizability to other meta-analytic settings requires systematic simulation.
Fifth, the sandwich variance estimator is known to be liberal when the number of clusters \(J\) is small, precisely the regime where Bayesian hierarchical models are most attractive. The regularity conditions of Chapter 11 ensure asymptotic validity as \(J \to \infty\), but for meta-analyses with \(J < 30\), the DER may be poorly estimated and the CCC correction may not achieve its nominal coverage targets. Small-sample corrections analogous to CR2 (Bell and McCaffrey 2002; Tipton 2015) for the Bayesian DER pipeline remain undeveloped.
Sixth, the DER is computed conditional on a chosen working correlation \(\rho_{\mathrm{work}}\). The DER values change — sometimes substantially — as \(\rho_{\mathrm{work}}\) varies. Sensitivity analysis over a grid of \(\rho\) values is recommended to assess the stability of the diagnostic conclusions, as discussed in the boundary analysis of Chapter 9.

title: 13  Discussion and Conclusions
text:
13.4 Future Directions
The limitations above suggest a natural research agenda, which I organize into six directions.
Simulation studies. A systematic Monte Carlo investigation varying \(J\) (number of studies), \(\bar{n}\) (average cluster size), \(K\) (number of covariates), within-study covariate structures (\(\gamma_k\) from 0 to 1), and degree of (E2) violation would establish the operating characteristics of the DER/CCC pipeline under realistic conditions. Of particular interest is the minimum \(J\) required for adequate CCC performance and the behavior of the conservation gap as design imbalance increases.
Non-Gaussian outcomes. Extending the isomorphism to exponential-family likelihoods would cover binary, count, and bounded outcomes. The key question is whether the parameter-free constant \(c\) in Theorem 1 survives when the log-likelihood involves a non-quadratic sufficient statistic. The estimating-equation approach of Lee (2026a) provides a practical template, but a formal isomorphism result for generalized linear mixed models remains open.
Multi-way clustering. Network meta-analysis introduces multiple clustering dimensions — study and treatment-contrast — that the two-level theory does not address. Extending the DER decomposition and conservation law to \(L\)-level hierarchies (\(L \geq 3\)) would require a system of conservation equations, one for each pair of adjacent levels, with level-specific shrinkage factors governing the redistribution dynamics.
Adaptive DER thresholds. The current five-tier classification uses fixed thresholds (0.95, 1.05, 2.0, 4.0). Treating the DER as a random variable with a posterior distribution — induced by the posterior uncertainty in the sandwich components — would allow probabilistic classification and data-adaptive threshold selection, potentially improving the sensitivity of the diagnostic in small-\(J\) settings.
Software implementation. An integrated R package that wraps the compute–classify–correct workflow around Stan-based meta-analytic models would lower the barrier to adoption. The package would extract MCMC draws, compute the marginal and conditional sandwiches, evaluate the DER tier for each parameter, apply CCC where warranted, and produce diagnostic plots — all as a post-processing step on a fitted Stan object.
Bayesian model averaging. The equivalence is established for a given working-covariance specification. Averaging over multiple working-covariance structures — CE, HE, CHE with different \(\omega^2\) — using Bayesian model averaging weights could produce DER-calibrated posteriors that are robust to working-model uncertainty at a level the current framework does not address.

title: 13  Discussion and Conclusions
text:
13.5 Conclusion
This book has established that Bayesian meta-analysis with dependent effect sizes and Bayesian survey inference under clustered sampling are, under five explicit conditions, not two methods but one, viewed from different disciplinary vantage points. The isomorphism map \(\mathcal{T}\) identifies the likelihoods (Theorem 1), the sandwich variances (Theorem 2), the posteriors (Corollary 1), and the calibrated posteriors (Corollary 2) — a chain visualized in Figure 4.1 through Figure 6.1. The DER framework (Theorem 3) transfers the survey design-effect diagnostic tradition to meta-analysis, revealing a conservation law that constrains how the design-effect budget is allocated across parameters.
The Calibrated Bayes program of Little (2004) and Little (2012) — Bayesian computation validated by frequentist coverage criteria — is now available to meta-analysis at the individual-parameter level. The DER provides the diagnostic; the CCC provides the correction; the conservation law provides the structural constraint. The entire apparatus operates as a post-processing step on existing MCMC output, at negligible computational cost.
The practical message is concise. For any Bayesian correlated-effects meta-analysis fitted in Stan: extract the MCMC draws; compute the cluster-level sandwich variance; form the DER for each parameter; where the DER exceeds the T1 threshold, apply the Cholesky Calibration Correction. The result is a set of Bayesian credible intervals that honor both the hierarchical structure of the model and the design-based reality of within-study dependence — the operational realization, for this class of models, of the Calibrated Bayes vision articulated by Little (2004) and Rubin (1984).
The open questions — general working covariance structures, non-normal likelihoods, multi-level hierarchies, finite-sample corrections — define a research program that bridges two communities with much to learn from each other. The foundation is in place. The dictionary has been written. The translation can begin.

title: Revision History
text:
Version History
This appendix documents the evolution of the theory book across seven major versions and provides a complete ledger of all corrections applied during the final verification and harmonization stages. The revision history is included for scholarly transparency, following the practice that mathematical monographs disclose the nature and scope of post-dissemination corrections.
Version 1 (Initial Structure). The foundational 12-chapter, four-part architecture was established. Part I defined the notation, model specifications, and exactness conditions (E1)–(E5). Part II contained the core equivalence results: the likelihood isomorphism (Theorem 1), sandwich equivalence (Theorem 2), and the posterior and CCC corollaries. Part III introduced the Design Effect Ratio framework, the conservation law, and the boundary analysis. Part IV addressed HE and CHE extensions, regularity conditions, and interpretive issues. All principal theorem statements were formulated in this version.
Version 2 (Feasibility Review). An independent mathematical review validated the overall soundness of the equivalence framework. The review confirmed that the CE core results were tractable and that HE and CHE extensions could be handled as scope expansions within the existing four-part structure. No reorganization of the chapter sequence was required.
Version 3 (Formal Proofs). Complete formal proofs were supplied for all theorems, corollaries, and supporting lemmas. The DER transfer theorem (Theorem 3) was established as a direct consequence of Theorems 1 and 2. Cross-references linking the proof steps to earlier definitions and conditions were consolidated throughout.
Version 4 (Full Content). The complete 12-chapter text was finalized. This version introduced the full DER variant taxonomy (marginal, profile, conditional, blockwise, element-wise), the conservation law derivation with the TSL15 empirical illustration, and the boundary case analysis for all four limiting regimes of Theorem 3(d). The Part IV chapters on HE/CHE extensions and regularity conditions were written in full.
Version 5 (Quality Control). A systematic quality-control pass was conducted. Notation was audited for consistency across all 12 chapters, all internal cross-references were verified, and the citation key format was standardized. Naming conventions were harmonized to use field-level terminology throughout.
Version 6 (Manuscript Harmonization). The theory book was brought into alignment with the companion manuscript undergoing second revision for JEBS. Five categories of updates were applied: (i) adoption of the broad CE definition, in which CE is characterized by \(\omega^2 = 0\) and individual-level covariates \(\mathbf{x}_{ij}\) are permitted; (ii) reframing of condition (E4) from “Study-Level Covariates” to “DER Simplification Condition,” removing it from the requirements for Theorems 1–2; (iii) migration of the DER tier classification to the asymmetric T0–T4 system (thresholds 0.95, 1.05, 2.0, 4.0) from Lee (2026); (iv) expansion of the bibliography from 9 to 27 BibTeX entries, with all inline text citations converted to @key format; and (v) updating of the conservation law empirical values to four-decimal precision (LHS = 6.1065, RHS = 5.6200, Gap = +8.7%). These changes are recorded as items 20–28 in the corrections ledger below.
Version 7 (Mathematical Verification). A line-by-line mathematical verification was performed across all 12 chapters. The review identified 19 targeted corrections (items 1–19 in the ledger), none of which constituted a critical error. The two highest-severity findings — the boundary-case proof for Theorem 3(d)(iii)–(iv) in 9  Boundary Cases and Edge Analysis and the positive-definiteness argument in Blockwise CCC — were fully rewritten. The remaining items involved tightening scope qualifiers, adding explicit formulas, or softening overly strong claims. The document compiled without errors and was deemed publication-ready upon completion of this version.
Version 8 (Publication Readiness). A comprehensive editorial pass transformed the document from a mathematical reference into a publication-ready monograph. Four categories of changes were applied: (i) metadata and configuration — the author field was set to JoonHo Lee, the subtitle was updated to “Complete Mathematical Theory,” and all references to collaborative AI verification were removed from the text; (ii) preface rewrite — the index page was rewritten as a scholarly introduction providing historical context (from classical fixed-effects meta-analysis through Pustejovsky–Tipton 2022), the five-part roadmap, and the intended audience, replacing the earlier change-log format; (iii) Part V addition — a new Discussion and Conclusion chapter (13  Discussion and Conclusions) was added, summarizing the principal contributions, stating limitations, and outlining five directions for future work; and (iv) narrative enhancement — 22 narrative paragraphs were inserted across 9 chapters (Ch. 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 12), providing chapter roadmaps, pre-theorem intuition, proof-strategy summaries, cross-chapter connections, and practical interpretations. No mathematical content was altered; all 16 files compiled without errors.
Version 9 (Pedagogical Visualizations). Thirteen MATLAB-generated pedagogical figures were integrated across 9 chapters, organized along three thematic spines: Covariance Geometry (fig01–fig04), Calibration and Correction (fig05–fig06, fig10), and DER Budget (fig07–fig09, fig11–fig13). The integration followed a six-phase process: (A) infrastructure setup, copying the V8 book into a new working directory and adding the MATLAB codebase; (B) automated MATLAB build of all 13 figures using headless rendering; (C) three rounds of multi-agent quality review identifying 23 improvements across figure code; (D) competitive implementation of all 23 fixes by two independent agents, with merge synthesis and before/after visual verification; (E) four-step book integration — Part I and II figures (fig01–04 into Ch. 2, 4, 5, 6), Part III figures (fig05–10 into Ch. 7, 8, 9), Part IV figures (fig11–13 into Ch. 10, 12), and a cross-reference/caption polish pass adding 6 caption rewrites and 6 new cross-chapter references; and (F) full Quarto build verification and visual QA. Each figure was inserted with a Quarto figure block ({#fig-id}), a contextual prose paragraph connecting the visualization to the relevant theorem or definition, and cross-references to related figures and equations. The final cross-reference network spans 21 @fig-* links across 9 chapters, with backward references creating pedagogical threads (eigenstructure, DEFF, equivalence chain, conservation). Quarto assigns chapter-based numbering: Figure 2.1 through Figure 12.1. No mathematical content was altered; all 16 files compiled without errors. These changes are recorded as items 34–46 in the corrections ledger below.

title: Revision History
text:
Verification Outcome
The V7 verification assessed all 12 chapters and produced the following severity classification for the 19 items (1–19):



Table 8: Severity classification of V7 corrections.










Severity
Count
Items




Critical (incorrect theorem conclusions)
0
—


High (incorrect mathematical claims)
2
1, 14


Medium (imprecise or ambiguous statements)
11
2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 11, 12, 13, 15, 17


Low (minor notation or wording)
6
4, 9, 10, 16, 18, 19






The two high-severity items were the Theorem 3(d) boundary-case proof (9  Boundary Cases and Edge Analysis, item 1) and the blockwise sandwich positive-definiteness argument (Blockwise CCC, item 14). Both were fully rewritten with corrected derivations. All remaining corrections involved scope tightening, notational precision, or language moderation, and none affected the validity of any stated result.
The V9 visualization integration (items 34–46) added 13 pedagogical figures, 13 contextual prose paragraphs, 6 caption refinements, and 6 cross-chapter backward references. No mathematical content was altered. The final book contains 5 Parts, 13 Chapters, 1 Appendix, 13 Figures, 29 BibTeX entries, and compiles without errors (16/16 files, 0 unresolved cross-references).




Lee, J. 2026. “Design Effect Ratios for Bayesian Survey Models: A Diagnostic Framework for Identifying Survey-Sensitive Parameters.” https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2603.07791.
